8tate of CaJifor1l.ia 
Fair Political Practices Commissio1l. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 9580.4 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
322·5660 
executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6-Ml 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322.¢4.4.4 
James C. Gross, Esq. 
Dobbs & Nielsen 
Suite 550, 1225 Eighth Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Gross: 
March 4, 1983 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, Our File No. A-83-028 
Your letter requests advice on behalf of J. Lewis Associates 
and its principal, Mr. Jonathan Lewis, as well as J. Lewis 
Associates' employees. 
The facts, as provided by you in your 
letter and our several telephone conversations, are as follows: 
FACTS 
Mr. Lewis and his firm engage in political consulting, 
lobbying and Political Action Committee ("PAC") management. 
Mr. Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and 
lobby on behalf of various clients. 
J. Lewis Associates ("JLA") 
has recently entered into a contract to provide consulting 
services to the Block Grant Advisory Task Force created by 
Section 16367 of the Government Code. 
This Task Force is 
designed to determine the availability and recommend the 
disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the 
states by the federal government under 42 U.S.C. Sections 9901, 
et~. JLA will help develop recommendations on all matters 
pertinent to block grants and will provide the Task Force with 
public policy development and will research information which 
will be used in the Task Force's presentations to the Legis-
lature. 
The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to 
various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide 
social services. 
More specifically, the federal government is currently 
making funds available to California and is planning to increase 
that funding in the future. 
These funds are to b~ contained in 
"block grants", with many fewer restrictions on their use 
have applied to "categorical" grants in the past. 
In order to 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 2 
determine priorities for allocation of these federal funds, the 
Legislature has established the Task Force which is to gather 
information as to (1) the amount of funds available; (2) what 
restrictions, if any, are placed upon their use; (3) what are 
the most pressing needs for these funds in California; and (4) 
what are the existing mechanisms for utilization and distri-
bution of these funds, including state and local agencies and 
non-profit organizations. 
Then the Task Force will propose 
priorities for allocation of these funds to best meet the needs 
as determined. 
A report, which includes recommendations for 
legislative action and establishment of public policy, will be 
forwarded by the Task Force to the Legislature as a blueprint 
for a program by the state for receiving and expending the 
federal block grant funds. 
The Block Grant Advisory Task Force 
makes no final decisions regarding the disposition of funds, but 
does make recommendations to the Legislature regarding fund 
availability and statewide needs. 
The Task Force's duties are 
more completely detailed in Government Code Section 16367(d) I 
enacted by Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, a copy of which is attached 
a s Exh i bit " A " • 
.. 
Under Government Code Section 16367.2(c) $135,000 is 
appropriated by the Legislature from the Federal Block Grant 
Fund to the Controller's office for disbursement to pay for 
administrative costs of the Task Force. 
Those are the funds to 
be paid to JLA in its conduct of the administrative activities 
of the Task Force. 
In effect, JLA's employees will be function-
ing as the staff for the Task Force. 
According to the contract between JLA and the Block Grant 
Advisory Task Force, JLA is to serve in a policy-making capacity 
and as a liaison between the Task Force and the Legislature. 
However, both JLA and the Task Force have agreed that JLA and 
its employees and representatives shall perform administrative 
and consulting tasks and no lobbying activities. 
(See attached 
agreement) . 
Employees of JLA lobby for a number of organizations. 
One 
of those organizations is the Jewish public Affairs Committee 
("JPAC"). 
JPAC is not eligible for any Block Grant funds. 
JPAC's only interest is in seeing that tne funds are equitably 
disbursed according to each community's needs. 
JLA's 
relationship with JPAC is in no way connected to or contingent 
upon its relationship with the Block Grant Advisory Task Force, 
and JLA has no intention of lobbying the Block Grant Advisory 
Task Force on behalf of JPAC. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 3 
JLA has disclosed its relationship with JPAC to all members 
of the Task Force and has disclosed its relationship with the 
Task Force to JPAC. 
It has advised both parties that it will 
disqualify itself in any decisions that affect JPAC or any of 
its members. 
QUESTIONS AND ANALYSES 
You have posed several questions based upon the foregoing 
facts which will be restated and answered in turn. 
Question 1: 
Is the Block Grant Advisory Task Force a "State 
Agency" under the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
The Task Force is a state agency. 
It is involved in 
performing the public's work, using public funds. 
Its members 
are all appointees of the Governor or the Legislature and 
include the Director of Finance, the Chair of the Senate Finance 
Committee and the Chair of the Assembly Ways and Means 
Committee. 
It is officially sanctioned to produce a work 
product on which the Legislature and the Governor will place 
heavy reliance. 
In your letter requesting advice, you have directed our 
attention to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18249, which defines 
whether a public entity .is state or local for purposes of the 
lobbying registration and reporting requirements of the 
political Reform Act (the !tAct")l/. 
(See, Opinion requested 
by James J. Vonk, 6 FPPC Opinions 4 at-S; No. 80-008, March 2, 
1981.) 
Because there are some lobbying questions involved here, 
we shall consider its applicability. 
Regulation 18249 provides 
as follows: 
An agency is a state agency within the provisions 
of Government Code Sections 82004 and 86100-86300 only 
if all the following criteria are met: 
(a) 
The agency is authorized by statute, 
executive order or the California Constitution. 
!/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All 
statutory references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise specif~ed. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 4 
(b) 
At least one voting member is an elected 
state officer or is appointed by an elected state 
officer or an agency official or a state agency. 
(c) 
The agency is financed in part by any 
state funds or is subject to appropriation in the 
state budget. 
(d) 
An area larger than one county is 
included in its jurisdiction. 
The Task Force is created by statute and its composition is 
set out in that statute. 
Section 16367. 
Clearly, the Task 
Force meets the criteria of Regulation l8249(a) and (b). 
Just 
as clearly, its jurisdiction is statewide, meeting the criteria 
of subdivision (d). 
In your letter, you have urged that the method of funding of 
the Task Force is not encompassed by subdivision (c) in that the 
ultimate funding source is the Federal Government and tbat the 
funds were not specifically appropriated in the state budget. 
We respectfully disagree. 
In the Vonk Opinion, supra at p.8, we 
stated, in footnote 3: 
The Commission's deliberations concerning 
subsection (c) indicate that it was intended to cover 
any funds which flow through the State Treasury and any 
funds which are raised pursuant to state authority, 
such as agricultural and industry assessments, in 
addition to regular state appropriations. 
Section 16367.2, which provides the funding for the Task 
Force, reads as follows: 
(a) 
There is hereby created in the State Treasury 
the Federal Block Grant Fund, to which there is 
allocated one hundred thirty-five thousand dollars 
($135,000) from those federal block grant funds 
designated by the Budget Act of 1982 for block grant 
administrative costs incurred by administering state 
agencies. 
(b) 
The appropriation by the Budget Act of 1982 
to each administering state agency for federal block 
grant administrative costs shall be reduced by a 
proportionate share for allocation to the Federal Block 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 5 
Grant Fund pursuant to subdivision (a), with the 
exception of the Education and Community Development 
block grants. 
(c) 
The sum of one hundred thirty-five thousand 
dollars ($135,000) is hereby appropriated from the 
Federal Block Grant Fund to the Controller for 
disbursement in support of the olock grant advisory 
task force under Section 16367 during the 1982-83 
fiscal year. 
It is the intent of the Legislature that there 
exist an operating expense budget to cover the 
operational costs of the block grant advisory task 
force required pursuant to Article 1.7 (commencing with 
Section 16366.1). 
These costs shall include, but are 
not limited to, staffing, operating expenses, per diem, 
public notice, and printing costs. 
(d) 
This section shall remain in effect only 
until July 1, 1984, and as of that date is repealed. 
A careful reading of the statutory provision makes it clear 
that these are funds which flow through tne State Treasury. 
The 
sum of $135,000 is appropriated by the Legislature and an 
expense budget is established. 
The fact that tne appropriation 
"sunsets" after one and one-half years does not alter the nature 
of the funding. 
It is always the case that when a new state 
agency or program will become operative on January first~/, 
the legislation creating it must contain an appropriation 
because the agency will not be included in the Budget Act for 
the current fiscal year. 
This does not mean that such an agency 
is not a "state agency" within the meaning of the Act. 
Further-
more, as specifically set forth in Section 16367.2(b) I 
the funds 
here are being reallocated from appropriations contained in the 
Budget Act of 1982 and, consequently, Chap_ 1343 acts as an 
amendment to the Budget Act of 1982. 
Conclusion 
Because of its duties and status, the Task Force is a state 
agency within the meaning of the Act generally. 
We further 
conclude that the Block Grant Advisory Task Force is a state, 
~I The appropriation contained in Section 16367.2 was 
enacted in Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, and became effective 
January 1, 1983. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4,1983 
Page 6 
rather than a local, agency within the meaning of tne lobbying 
provisions of the Act. 
Question 2: 
Are the members of the Task Force and the employees of 
J. Lewis Associates who are working for the Task Force "public 
officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
"Public official" as defined in Section 82048, includes 
every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or 
local government agency. 
Since the Task Force is a 
stat~ 
agency, members of the Task Force are public officials under 
Section 82004. 
Employees of JLA who work for the Task Force are 
either employees or consultants (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18700 (a) (2) 
0 f a state agency and hence "public 
officials" under Section 82048. 
Question 3: 
Are J. Lewis Associates' employees who are working on the 
Task Force project subject to the Conflict of Interest 
Provisions of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
Section 87100 provides that: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
(Emphasis added.) 
Consequently, as public officials the members and staff of the 
Task Force are covered by the Conflict of Interest provisions of 
the Act requiring disqualification where appropriate. 
Question 4: 
Are the Task Force's members and staff covered by the 
Legislature's Conflict of Interest Code? 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 7 
Analysis 
As you have pointed out in your letter, the Legislature's 
Code will cover the Task Force (under Section 3), if the Task 
Force is considered to be a "joint committee." 
If that is the 
case, then the non-clerical staff and the members of the Task 
Force not already covered by Article 2 of Chapter Seven of the 
Act would be designated employees under that section. 
However, 
this would be so only if it is determined that the Task Force is 
a Joint Committee. 
That is a question which should be addressed 
to the Legislature or perhaps Legislative Counsel. 
If the Task 
Force is not covered by the Legislature's Conflict of Interest 
Code, then it should adopt its own Code with a list of desig-
nated employees. 
We are prepared to assist in expediting this 
process. 
However, you should remember that the disqualification 
provisions of Section 87100 apply whether or not the Task Force 
is currently covered by a Code. 
Question 5: 
What conflict of interest issues are raised under Chapter 7 
of the Act by JLA's relationship with JPAC and the Task Force? 
Analysis 
In order for there to be a conflict of interest under 
Chapter 7 of the Act (Sections 87100, et seq.) requiring 
disqualification by an official, there-mu~first be a financial 
interest. 
Section 87103 defines when an official has a 
financial interest. 
The pertinent portion is set forth below. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms available to the 
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in 
value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 8 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
The Jewish Public Affairs Council is a source of income to 
J. Lewis Associates. 
However, it mayor may not be a source of 
income to individual employees of JLA. 
Almost assuredly it is a 
source of income to Mr. Lewis; we assume he owns more than 10% 
of JLA. 
Pursuant to Sections 82030(a) and 87103(c), if his pro 
rata share of JLA'S gross receipts from JPAC was $250 or more 
during the preceding 12-month period, then he would have a 
financial interest in JPAC, which could lead to disqualification. 
We have no information as to other employees to determine 
whether JPAC is a source of income to them. 
However, if an 
employee owns a 10% or greater share of JLA, then JPAC may be a 
source of income to that employee under Sections 82030(a) and 
87103{c). 
Moreover, even if JPAC is not a source of income, JLA 
is a source of income for each of the employees, as well as for 
Mr. Lewis. 
Thus, if a decision affected JPAC to such an extent 
that it resulted in a material financial effect on JLA, dis-
qualification would be required. 
Given the fact that JPAC is 
not eligible for Block Grant funds, and that JLA'S relationship 
with JPAC "is in no way connected to or contingent upon" JLA's 
relationship with the Task Force, it is not reasonably foresee-
able that any Task Force decision will have a material financial 
effect (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1» 
on 
JPAC, or on JLA as a result of its relationship to JPAC. 
In any 
event, JLA and its associates voluntarily have agreed to dis-
qualify themselves from any decision which could affect JPAC. 
However, for Mr. Lewis (and any other 10% owner of JLA) , we 
must also consider the provision in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3) (B) which provides that if there is a "nexus" between 
the purpose for which he receives income from JPAC and the 
governmental decisions he makes for the Task Force, then there 
is a material financial effect. 
He will be required to 
disqualify himself whenever such a "nexus" is present and the 
effect on JPAC is distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally.11 
Mr. Lewis has already agreed to do this. 
For 
11 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 for a discussion of 
the application of the "public generallyn concept. 
Here the 
jurisdiction is the entire State of California. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983 
Page 9 
further discussion of the "nexus" principle, please see the 
enclosed advice letters to Jeffrey Gunther (No. A-82-055) and 
Linda Best (NO. A-81-032) • 
Conclusion 
There does not appear to be any likelihood of conflict of 
interest disqualification for JLA employees based upon the fact 
that JPAC is a lobbying client of JLA. 
However, Mr. Lewis (and 
any other 10% owner of JLA) may have a conflict requiring 
disqualification as to any decisions meeting the "nexus" test 
with respect to JPAC. 
Question 6: 
Must JLA report the Task Force as a lobbying client? 
Analysis 
Since the Task Force is a state agency within the meaning of 
Regulation 18249, it follows that Section 86300(a) applies 
here. 
This section excludes persons lobbying for state agencies 
from most of the requirements and restrictions placed on other 
lobbyists, specifically exempting from registration and 
reporting. 
Consequently, neither JLA nor its lobbyist employees 
need to register the Task Force as a lobbying client. 
Conclusion 
J. Lewis Associates need not file an authorization from the 
Task Force to lobby on its behalf and need not report the Task 
Force as a client. 
We trust that this letter has adequately responded to the 
questions raised by you. 
Should you have further questions 
regarding this matter, you may contact me at 916/322-5901. 
REL: km 
Sincerely,

0 f a state agency and hence "public

to the Legislature or perhaps Legislative Counsel.

nated employees.

The sum of one hundred thirty-five thousand 
dollars ($135,000) is hereby appropriated from the 
Federal Block Grant Fund to the Controller for 
disbursement in support of the olock grant advisory 
task force under Section 16367 during the 1982-83 
fiscal year. 
It is the intent of the Legislature that there 
exist an operating expense budget to cover the 
operational costs of the block grant advisory task 
force required pursuant to Article 1.7 (commencing with 
Section 16366.1). 
These costs shall include, but are 
not limited to, staffing, operating expenses, per diem, 
public notice, and printing costs. 
(d) 
This section shall remain in effect only 
until July 1, 1984, and as of that date is repealed. 
A careful reading of the statutory provision makes it clear 
that these are funds which flow through tne State Treasury. 
The 
sum of $135,000 is appropriated by the Legislature and an 
expense budget is established. 
The fact that tne appropriation 
"sunsets" after one and one-half years does not alter the nature 
of the funding. 
It is always the case that when a new state 
agency or program will become operative on January first~/, 
the legislation creating it must contain an appropriation 
because the agency will not be included in the Budget Act for 
the current fiscal year. 
This does not mean that such an agency 
is not a "state agency" within the meaning of the Act. 
Further-
more, as specifically set forth in Section 16367.2(b) I 
the funds 
here are being reallocated from appropriations contained in the 
Budget Act of 1982 and, consequently, Chap_ 1343 acts as an 
amendment to the Budget Act of 1982.

QUESTION: Is the Block Grant Advisory Task Force a "State 
Agency" under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Because of its duties and status, the Task Force is a state 
agency within the meaning of the Act generally. 
We further 
conclude that the Block Grant Advisory Task Force is a state, 
~I The appropriation contained in Section 16367.2 was 
enacted in Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, and became effective 
January 1, 1983. 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4,1983  
rather than a local, agency within the meaning of tne lobbying 
provisions of the Act. 
Question 2: 
Are the members of the Task Force and the employees of 
J. Lewis Associates who are working for the Task Force "public 
officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
"Public official" as defined in Section 82048, includes 
every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or 
local government agency. 
Since the Task Force is a 
stat~ 
agency, members of the Task Force are public officials under 
Section 82004. 
Employees of JLA who work for the Task Force are 
either employees or consultants (as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18700 (a) (2) 
0 f a state agency and hence "public 
officials" under Section 82048. 
Question 3: 
Are J. Lewis Associates' employees who are working on the 
Task Force project subject to the Conflict of Interest 
Provisions of the Political Reform Act? 
Analysis 
Section 87100 provides that: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
(Emphasis added.) 
Consequently, as public officials the members and staff of the 
Task Force are covered by the Conflict of Interest provisions of 
the Act requiring disqualification where appropriate. 
Question 4: 
Are the Task Force's members and staff covered by the 
Legislature's Conflict of Interest Code? 


James C. Gross, Esq. 
March 4, 1983  
Analysis 
As you have pointed out in your letter, the Legislature's 
Code will cover the Task Force (under Section 3), if the Task 
Force is considered to be a "joint committee." 
If that is the 
case, then the non-clerical staff and the members of the Task 
Force not already covered by Article 2 of Chapter Seven of the 
Act would be designated employees under that section. 
However, 
this would be so only if it is determined that the Task Force is 
a Joint Committee. 
That is a question which should be addressed 
to the Legislature or perhaps Legislative Counsel. 
If the Task 
Force is not covered by the Legislature's Conflict of Interest 
Code, then it should adopt its own Code with a list of desig-
nated employees. 
We are prepared to assist in expediting this 
process. 
However, you should remember that the disqualification 
provisions of Section 87100 apply whether or not the Task Force 
is currently covered by a Code. 
Question 5: 
What conflict of interest issues are raised under Chapter 7 
of the Act by JLA's relationship with JPAC and the Task Force? 
Analysis 
In order for there to be a conflict of interest under 
Chapter 7 of the Act (Sections 87100, et seq.) requiring 
disqualification by an official, there-mu~first be a financial 
interest. 
Section 87103 defines when an official has a 
financial interest. 
The pertinent portion is set forth below. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:

BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 9580.4 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 (916) 322·5662 Administration 322·5660 executive/legal 322·5901 Enforcement 322-6-Ml Statements of Economic Interest 322.¢4.4.4 James C. Gross, Esq. Dobbs & Nielsen Suite 550, 1225 Eighth Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Mr. Gross: March 4, 1983 Re: Your Request for Written Advice, Our File No. A-83-028 Your letter requests advice on behalf of J. Lewis Associates and its principal, Mr. Jonathan Lewis, as well as J. Lewis Associates' employees. The facts, as provided by you in your letter and our several telephone conversations, are as follows: FACTS Mr. Lewis and his firm engage in political consulting, lobbying and Political Action Committee ("PAC") management. Mr. Lewis and some of his employees are registered lobbyists and lobby on behalf of various clients. J. Lewis Associates ("JLA") has recently entered into a contract to provide consulting services to the Block Grant Advisory Task Force created by Section 16367 of the Government Code. This Task Force is designed to determine the availability and recommend the disposition of federal block grant funds made available to the states by the federal government under 42 U.S.C. Sections 9901, et~. JLA will help develop recommendations on all matters pertinent to block grants and will provide the Task Force with public policy development and will research information which will be used in the Task Force's presentations to the Legis- lature. The federal funds will eventually be disbursed to various local agencies and non-profit organizations that provide social services. More specifically, the federal government is currently making funds available to California and is planning to increase that funding in the future. These funds are to b~ contained in "block grants", with many fewer restrictions on their use have applied to "categorical" grants in the past. In order to James C. Gross, Esq. March 4, 1983 Page 2 determine priorities for allocation of these federal funds, the Legislature has established the Task Force which is to gather information as to (1) the amount of funds available; (2) what restrictions, if any, are placed upon their use; (3) what are the most pressing needs for these funds in California; and (4) what are the existing mechanisms for utilization and distri- bution of these funds, including state and local agencies and non-profit organizations. Then the Task Force will propose priorities for allocation of these funds to best meet the needs as determined. A report, which includes recommendations for legislative action and establishment of public policy, will be forwarded by the Task Force to the Legislature as a blueprint for a program by the state for receiving and expending the federal block grant funds. The Block Grant Advisory Task Force makes no final decisions regarding the disposition of funds, but does make recommendations to the Legislature regarding fund availability and statewide needs. The Task Force's duties are more completely detailed in Government Code Section 16367(d) I enacted by Chap. 1343, Stats. 1982, a copy of which
